Section 7.15
THE E2 MECHANISM OF DEHYDROHALOGENATION OF ALKYL HALIDES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

E2: Concerted Elimination
Most eliminations of alkyl halides occur by the E2 mechanism (E1 is rarely synthetically useful)
E2 is a single-step mechanism in which deprotonation and leaving-group departure occur simultaneously
The slow step is bimolecular; the rate depends on [ Base − ] and [RX ]
Rate = k [Alkyl halide] [Base]
A strong base (such as alkoxide) is typically used
Reaction Coordinate Diagram

Reactivity Trends in E2
Reactivity increases with leaving-group ability. Faster E2 is associated with:
Weaker C–X bond
More stable X −
Tertiary alkyl halides react most rapidly, followed by secondary and primary halides

Section 7.16
ANTI ELIMINATION IN E2 REACTIONS: STEREOELECTRONIC EFFECTS

E2 Stereochemistry: Interesting Results
An axial Br eliminates readily in the presence of strong base, but an equatorial Br does not

E2 Stereochemistry: Interesting Results-1
This result and others suggest that the deprotonated H and leaving group must be anti in E2 eliminations

Stereoelectronic Effects in E2
Why must –H and –X be anti ? The new π bond in the product arises from side-on overlap of p orbitals, which is best in the anti geometry
The C–H σ orbital overlaps ideally with the C–X σ ∗ orbital in this arrangement .
This effect, grounded in orbital overlap and the spatial arrangements of orbitals, is called a stereoelectronic effect
Backside attack in S N 2 is another example !

Stereoselectivity for ( E ) Products
Transition states with –H and –X anti also help explain the selectivity of E2 for ( E ) or trans products
The minor 2-alkene derives from the transition state in which the alkyl groups are gauche :

Stereoselectivity for ( E ) Products-1
Transition states with –H and –X anti also help explain the selectivity of E2 for ( E ) or trans products
The major 2-alkene derives from the transition state in which the alkyl groups are anti :

Section 7.17
ISOTOPE EFFECTS AND THE E2 MECHANISM

Kinetic Isotope Effects
Reactions involving cleavage of an R–H bond exhibit kinetic isotope effects
A C–D bond is slightly stronger (12 kj / mol ) than a C–H bond, so the activation energy for reaction of C–D is slightly higher than the activation energy for reaction of C–H
We observe this effect in kinetic data when C–H(D) cleavage occurs in the rate-determining step
When k C -H / k C -D ≫ 1 , we conclude that the C–H bond is involved in the slow step
What should we expect in an isotopic study of E2 elimination ?

KIEs in E2 Elimination
In the slow (and only!) step of E2, a C–H bond breaks. Thus, k C-H / k C-D should be greater than 1
In fact , k C-H / k C-D = 6.7 for the reaction below
This result supports the concerted mechanism of E2 (along with previously discussed kinetic data )
Kinetic isotope effect studies are useful for determining whether C–H cleavage occurs in the slow step of a reaction mechanism .

Section 7.18
THE E1 MECHANISM OF DEHYDROHALOGENATION OF ALKYL HALIDES

E1 Elimination
Deprotonation and leaving-group departure may not be concerted—particularly when the base is weak
In the presence of a weak base, an alkyl halide may lose the halide to form a carbocation before deprotonation

E1 Elimination-1
In the presence of a weak base, an alkyl halide may lose the halide to form a carbocation before deprotonation
In the second step, solvent deprotonates the carbocation at a β -carbon , forming the product alkene

Reactivity Trends and Selectivity
Zaitsev’s rule is followed. The most substituted alkene is formed preferentially
Tertiary alkyl halides react fastest. They form the most stable carbocations

An Important Analogy
Note that E1 dehydrohalogenation and acid-catalyzed dehydration of alcohols are very similar!
Both reactions share a common intermediate and differ only in the structure of the leaving group

Section 7.19
SUBSTITUTION AND ELIMINATION AS COMPETING REACTIONS

Competing Reactions
Substitution and elimination involve the same substrates and reaction conditions
Using previously discussed reactivity trends, we can predict whether substitution or elimination will occur given alkyl halide and Lewis base structures

General Principles
Alkyl halides generally undergo elimination in the presence of Lewis bases. For example,
Only under certain circumstances will substitution predominate:
Minimal steric hindrance in the alkyl halide and base
Weak Brønsted basicity (but substantial Lewis basicity) in the nucleophile
Brønsted or Lewis Base?

Minimal Steric Hindrance
For unhindered primary alkyl halides, S N 2 substitution predominates no matter the strength of the base
However, a sterically hindered base can tip the scales in favor of elimination !
Both the electrophile and the nucleophile must be sterically un hindered to promote S N 2 substitution .

Low Brønsted Basicity
Some nucleophiles are weak Brønsted bases despite being good Lewis bases; these nucleophiles engage in substitution selectively
Anionic nucleophiles ( A − ) whose conjugate acids (HA) have p K a <15 are the classic examples:
Thiolates ( HS − , RS − )
Cyanide ( CN − )
Azide ( N 3 − )
Neutral solvent nucleophiles such as water and alcohols engage in substitution exclusively with tertiary halides

Section 7.20
ELIMINATION REACTIONS OF SULFONATES

Eliminations of Alkyl Sulfonates
Sulfonates are pseudohalides and undergo many of the same reactions as halides
Elimination of alkyl sulfonates follows the same reactivity trends and selectivity rules as dehydrohalogenation:
The most substituted product is favored
The trans product is favored over the cis product
